transcript.clean_text: All the drama of the Giro d'Italia is live and on demand each day on GCN Plus. Another day and more rain, I'm afraid. The longest day of the Giro d'Italia and a longer day for some teams. It was hard work for Soudal Quick-Step, having to say goodbye to four riders, but there were good spirits in other camps, despite the fact that eight in total would not start. The COVID pandemic still going in professional cycling. 219 km, the longest stage of the Giro d'Italia taking in three regions, leaving Tuscany and Camaiore going to Liguria and finishing in Piemonte. A finish in Tortona, of course, the city synonymous with the late great Fausto Coppi. Stage 11 of the Giro, halfway through, and six riders made it into the break of the day. Only three or four kilometers were required to get the right ingredients. Those behind in sprint teams were determined to make this a day for the fast finishers. Astana, Alpecin-Deceuninck, Cavendish, Trek riding for Mads Pedersen, and Movistar for Fernando Gaviria contributing to the early pace setting as they tried to set up a sprint. The intermediate sprint, the break had their fun, and Jonathan Milan was looking strong, beating Mads Pedersen to more points in the Maglia Ciclamino competition and extending his lead in the points classification. Behind in the peloton, 78 km remained. The gap was down. Kaden Groves was suffering again with illness, but then this happened. A relatively quiet day for the peloton, but on a slippery descent as the rain started to come down, the Maglia Rosa Geraint Thomas went down. Roglič hitting the deck, Covili the first man to go down. Sivakov injured, too, but Tao Geoghegan Hart was the man, I'm afraid, who was out of the Giro d'Italia with injury. Oscar Rodriguez would also follow. Geoghegan Hart taken away to hospital. Rodriguez's injuries also looking rather worrying. The race continued, as it always does. And Caja Rural were the ones to try and make it hard for the thicker-leg sprinters. The breakaway still just about surviving. A few sprinters briefly distanced, but they'd get back on with 30 km to go. There'd only be 5 km remaining when the last man in the break was caught. That was Lawrence Rex. And we were expecting a sprint finish. A crash behind with just under 2 km to go took out Fernando Gaviria and a few others. Henok Mulubrhan also hitting the ground. And and those who survived at the front, with no GC differences given the crash was inside the last 3 km, would then sprint it out. Pedersen leading it out. Cavendish looking good. Ackermann reborn on the right-hand side. Milan coming from nowhere. Four coming to the line. It needed a photo finish, though, to separate Pascal Ackermann from Jonathan Milan. Cavendish in third. Pedersen would take fourth. A big sprint. Cavendish looking brilliant. Pedersen regular as ever. Milan, the revelation of the Giro. But Ackermann winning a stage of the Giro for the first time since 2019. A superb result for the man who hardly has any team support at this race. Gibbons, the only man sent to look after him, did a fantastic job. And Ackermann top of the tree. Ackermann, Milan, Cavendish, Pedersen, and Oldani with Albanese, Mayrhofer, Ballerini, Consonni, and Marit in the top 10. Geraint Thomas in the maglia rosa again, but without the man who was third place, Tao So, it's now Thomas 2 seconds in front of Roglič, Almeida at 22, Leknessund in fourth, Caruso, then Kämna, Dunbar, and the rest in the top 10. The GC really gets going now in terms of terrain to challenge. A tricky stage, a lumpy stage, and going to Rivoli for stage 12 before the high mountains await from Friday onwards. 179 km with the late Colle Braida making perhaps terrain for those who want to attack and move up in the GC. The Giro d'Italia continues. There's been lots of drama on and off the bike in the last few days. Let's see if the GC fight really lights up the good racing we all want to see.
